title: The Case of Absolute Value
introTutorialText:
Welcome to the tutorial! This is where you'll learn the basics of getting the hang at being a lawyer at the Syntax Criminal Court! To 
start, let's familiarize ourselves with the interface you'll be using to disect each case. Starting off, each case will follow this UI:
descTutorialText:
The given function above claims to follow the specifications on the right. Select 
the two test cases that best interrogate this claim. Will they confirm the function's innocence 
or expose its guilt?
description: This function claims to return the non-negative absolute value of any number.

testFeedback.feedback: This test covers the case where the input is a positive number
This test covers the case where the input is a negative number
This test is redundant because it tests the same case as test 1 (positive number)

missedEvidenceExplanation: We never tested for n=0, which is an important edge case for this function
closingStatement: The function correctly handles positive and negative numbers, but it fails to account for the edge case of zero. Therefore, the correct verdict is "not guilty" because the function is mostly correct but has a significant oversight.

title: The False Start
description: This function is supposed to greet a user by adding 'Hello ' before their name.

testFeedback.feedback: This clearly shows the names are smashed together without a space.
The input has a leading space (' Bob'), which makes the function appear correct.
Even with no name, we expect a trailing space after Hello.
This is just another example of the same concatenation error.

missedEvidenceExplanation: The function fails to provide its own space, relying on the user to include one in their input string.
closingStatement: Guilty! The defendant failed to include the necessary space character in the return string, leading to unreadable greetings.

title: The One-Track Mind
description: I check if a number is even. I return True for evens and False for odds.

testFeedback.feedback: Testing an even number hides the missing logic for odd numbers.
CRITICAL! This shows that for odd numbers, the function returns 'None' instead of 'False'.
Zero is even, so this test also hides the incomplete logic.
Confirms that the function simply doesn't know how to handle non-even numbers.

missedEvidenceExplanation: Every function needs an exit path for every condition; this one only had a path for success.
closingStatement: Guilty! The defendant was so focused on being 'Even' that they completely forgot how to identify 'Odd' numbers, returning 'None' instead.

title: The Overzealous Adder
description: I add two positive numbers. If either is 0 or less, I return 0.

testFeedback.feedback: This looks correct, but it doesn't test the 'invalid input' requirement.
Exposes that the function returns a string 'Error' instead of the required number 0.
Shows that even zero is handled by returning a string rather than the requested integer.
Another standard positive case that ignores the specific error requirement.

missedEvidenceExplanation: The function's description specifically asked for a return value of 0, but the code returned a string, which could break math operations.
closingStatement: Guilty! While it can add, the defendant failed to follow the specific error-handling protocol, returning text when a number was expected.

title: The Listless Guardian
description: I return the first item in any list. If the list is empty, I return 'None'.

evidencePool.label: first([]) -> None
evidencePool.actualOutput: IndexError: list index out of range

testFeedback.feedback: Works fine when there is data, but hides the danger.
SMOKING GUN! The function crashes completely when given an empty list.
Hides the lack of a safety check.
Just more proof that it works... until it doesn't.

missedEvidenceExplanation: Accessing items[0] without checking the length of the list is a common cause of program crashes.
closingStatement: Guilty! The defendant claimed to handle empty lists but actually crashed the entire court on the first empty input!

title: The Capital Crime
description: I ensure passwords are secure. They must be 8 characters long AND contain at least one uppercase letter.

testFeedback.feedback: Proves the function ignores the 'uppercase' requirement entirely.
This password is valid, so the function appears to work.
Correctly fails for length, but doesn't test the uppercase bug.
Just another case of the length check working.

missedEvidenceExplanation: To find this bug, you must test a password that is long enough but lacks an uppercase letter.
closingStatement: Guilty! The security guard was only looking at the size of the password, letting weak, lowercase passwords right through the gate!
